name: immudb Immutable Database API
what_it_does: Open-source immutable database with gRPC and HTTP REST API — enabling compliance, financial, and security teams to store cryptographically verifiable, tamper-evident records of transactions, audit logs, and events, with built-in cryptographic proofs (Merkle tree verification), SQL and key-value storage, and the ability to prove that data has not been altered since insertion.
use_cases: Storing immutable audit logs via immudb for compliance and regulatory record-keeping
Recording financial transactions with cryptographic proof via immudb for tamper-evident ledger
Building tamper-proof event logs via immudb for security incident investigation
Storing configuration change history via immudb for DevSecOps configuration auditing
Compliance data archival via immudb for GDPR, SOX, HIPAA audit trail requirements
not_for: General-purpose OLTP database workloads requiring updates/deletes (immudb is immutable — no deletes)
High-frequency write workloads requiring microsecond latency (immudb optimizes for verification over speed)
Replacing traditional RDBMS for mutable application data
best_when: A fintech, healthcare, or regulated industry company needs cryptographically verifiable immutable records — where immudb provides tamper-evident audit logs that can be mathematically proven to be unaltered.
avoid_when: You need mutable records, high-frequency updates, or a general-purpose application database.

auth.notes: JWT token authentication. Login via gRPC or HTTP API to get JWT. Username/password for initial login. JWT token in Authorization: Bearer header. User management via immuadmin CLI. No OAuth2. No fine-grained scopes per table (database-level access control). Codenotary Cloud: SaaS hosted version with API key auth. Self-hosted: user/password → JWT flow.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open source (Apache 2.0) — free self-hosted; Codenotary Cloud has free tier
pricing.paid_tiers: Codenotary Cloud from $99/month; Enterprise support via Codenotary
pricing.notes: Swiss company (Codenotary, Zug). Founded 2019. Created by Moshe Bar (founding CEO) and Dennis Zimmer. Go-based. Apache 2.0 license. GitHub: codenotary/immudb (8,600+ stars). World's first immutable database using Merkle tree proofs. Key-value and SQL storage modes. gRPC primary API + HTTP gateway. SDKs: Go, Java, Python, .NET, Node.js, Ruby, PHP. Codenotary Cloud: managed hosted immudb. Use cases: GDPR compliance, SOX audit trails, financial ledgers. CAS (Content Addressable Storage) for artifact signing (Codenotary Trustcenter). Not a blockchain — but cryptographically equivalent tamper-evidence.

performance.rate_limits: Self-hosted — no limits; Codenotary Cloud: plan-dependent

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: gRPC primary API + HTTP REST gateway. JWT token auth. Go/Java/Python/.NET/Node/Ruby/PHP SDKs. Apache 2.0 open source. Self-hosted or Codenotary Cloud. Immutable — no deletes/updates. Merkle tree proofs for verification.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: All reads fully idempotent. Writes are append-only (immutable) — naturally idempotent since data cannot be changed. Verification proofs idempotent for same transaction ID.
known_agent_gotchas: IMMUTABLE — no UPDATE or DELETE operations; only INSERT/GET/SCAN; design your schema accordingly
JWT token obtained via login endpoint — must refresh token before expiry in long-running agents
gRPC is primary API — HTTP REST gateway available but may have feature lag vs gRPC
Merkle proof verification requires cryptographic library support — use provided SDKs for verification
SQL mode and key-value mode have different APIs — choose at database creation time
Data cannot be deleted — GDPR right-to-erasure requires using Codenotary's privacy features

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS configurable (must enable for production). JWT auth. Immutability is the core security property — cryptographic proof of data integrity. Apache 2.0 open source — auditable. Swiss company (Zug) — strong privacy jurisdiction. Merkle tree proofs enable third-party verification. No OAuth2 scope granularity. GDPR note: right-to-erasure conflicts with immutability — use privacy features.
